text: Last class you opened the deal and proposed terms politely with would and could.
Today the sponsor pushes back: How do you handle an objection and close the deal?
Objection
Acknowledge
Deal-breaker
Concession
Budget
Reconsider
Guarantee
Stick to
Finalize
Sign off
On board
Trade-off
If we lowered the fee, would you sign for four seasons?
If we added two categories, would you reconsider?
If you kept our logo, we could give you an extra category.
I understand your concern. That is a fair point.
Honestly, your yearly price is too high for our budget. That is our main objection.
I understand your concern. That is a fair point. If we spread the payment across the season, would that work better for your budget?
That helps. But we also want your logo off the training kit. For us, that is important.
The logo on the kit is a deal-breaker for us. But if you kept our logo, we could give you an extra category as a concession.
An extra category? Let me reconsider that. Yes, I think I am on board.
Wonderful. So, do we have a deal? Shall we finalize the terms today and sign off?
That is a deal-breaker, but we could offer a concession.
So, do we have a deal? Shall we finalize the terms today?
I cannot guarantee that, but I can offer a follow-up review.

Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time we opened a negotiation and proposed terms. Today we go to the hard part: the other side pushes back, raises objections, and you have to close the deal. So here is some advice. First, never fight an objection. When the sponsor says the price is too high, or this clause is a deal breaker, stay calm and acknowledge their concern. Say, I understand your concern, or, that is a fair point. This lowers the tension. Second, ask a question to find the real problem behind the objection. Often the budget is not the only issue. Third, to persuade, use the second conditional. This is a softer, more hypothetical way to make an offer. You say, if we lowered the yearly fee, would you sign for four seasons, or, if we added two more categories, would you reconsider. Notice the pattern: if plus past tense, and would plus the base verb. It sounds polite and it makes the other side imagine the deal. Fourth, offer a small concession to get something bigger in return, but protect your deal breakers, the points you cannot give up. And finally, when you sense agreement, move to close. Say, so, do we have a deal, or, shall we finalize the terms today. Get them to sign off while the mood is good. Remember, Doctor, an objection is not a no. It is a door. Open it with a good question and a smart hypothetical offer. Good luck closing the deal.

Let me tell you how I closed my toughest sponsorship deal for Corinthians. We had already proposed our terms, but the marketing director came back with three objections. First, he said our yearly price was too high for his budget. I did not argue. I said, I understand your concern, that is a fair point, and then I asked a question to find the real issue. It turned out the timing was the problem, not the total value. So I tried a hypothetical offer. I said, if we spread the payment across the season, would that work better for your budget. He liked that idea. His second objection was a real deal breaker for us. He wanted our logo off the training kit, and that was a point I could not give up. So I protected it, but I offered a small concession somewhere else. I said, if we gave you exclusive rights in one extra category, would you keep our logo on the kit. He reconsidered, and he agreed. His last concern was a guarantee about audience numbers. I could not promise exact figures, so I stayed honest, but I offered a follow up review after six months. Slowly, every objection turned into a question, and every question turned into a small agreement. When I felt the mood was right, I moved to close. I said, so, do we have a deal, shall we finalize the terms today. He smiled and signed off. The whole time, my best tool was the second conditional. If we did this, would you do that. It let me test ideas without promising anything, and it kept both of us comfortable until the deal was done.

So, do we have a deal? Shall we finalize the terms today?
I cannot give a guarantee, but I can offer a follow-up review.

Here is how to handle an objection and close. First, stay calm and acknowledge the concern. Then, ask a question to find the real problem behind the objection. After that, make a hypothetical offer with the second conditional. Next, protect your deal-breaker but trade a small concession. Finally, when the other side is on board, move to close and finalize the deal.
I understand your concern. That is a fair point.
That is a deal-breaker, but we could offer a concession.
I cannot guarantee that, but I can offer a follow-up review.
